title: Construct BT from Preorder and Inorder

statement.markdown:
Given two integer arrays `preorder` and `inorder` where `preorder` is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and `inorder` is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

You may assume that duplicates do not exist in the tree.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain the elements of the preorder traversal.
The next line contains a single integer representing the size of the inorder traversal (which is also N).
The next N lines contain the elements of the inorder traversal.
statement.outputFormat: The preorder traversal of the constructed binary tree, with node values separated by spaces.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.
Node values in the preorder array
Node values in the inorder array

hints.text: The first element in the preorder array is the root of the tree. Inorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the root, then the right subtree.
You can find the index of the root in the inorder array. Elements to the left of this index are in the left subtree, and elements to the right are in the right subtree.
The preorder array gives you the root and then the left subtree nodes, followed by the right subtree nodes. You can recursively apply this process to construct the tree.

editorial.title: Constructing a Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Sequences
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that in a preorder traversal, the root node is visited first, and then the left and right subtrees are traversed. In an inorder traversal, the left subtree is visited, then the root node, and finally the right subtree. By matching these two sequences, we can uniquely identify the root node and recursively construct the tree.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible binary trees and checking if their preorder and inorder traversals match the given sequences. This approach is correct but extremely slow, with a time complexity of O(2^N) or worse, because there are exponentially many possible binary trees.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a recursive divide-and-conquer strategy based on the properties of preorder and inorder traversals.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution works by identifying the root node from the preorder sequence, finding its position in the inorder sequence, and then recursively constructing the left and right subtrees. This approach is known to have a time complexity of O(N) and a space complexity of O(N), making it much more efficient.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is when the tree is empty or has one node, which is straightforward. For larger trees, if the root node is correctly identified and the left and right subtrees are correctly constructed by recursive application of the algorithm, then the entire tree is correctly constructed. The preorder and inorder properties ensure that the root node and the subtrees are correctly aligned.
editorial.dryRun: Testing with sample inputs, such as preorder = [3, 9, 20, 15, 7] and inorder = [9, 3, 15, 20, 7], helps verify that the optimal solution produces the correct binary tree structure.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly assuming that the preorder or inorder sequences can be directly used to construct the tree without considering the properties of binary tree traversals.
Failing to recursively apply the construction process to the subtrees.
Not properly handling the base cases, such as an empty tree or a tree with a single node.
Misinterpreting the positions of nodes in the preorder and inorder sequences.
Overlooking the need to use a data structure like a hash map to efficiently find the position of a node in the inorder sequence.

functionSignature.java: public TreeNode buildTree(int[] preorder, int[] inorder);
functionSignature.python: def buildTree(self, preorder: List[int], inorder: List[int]) -> TreeNode:
